prompt: Write a letter from James Madison to George Washington in 1788, urging him to accept the presidency, where Madison must appeal to Washington's sense of duty while privately knowing that Washington's stature is essential to the Constitution's political survival in a way no abstract argument can replace.
ground_truth_signal: Deferential but substantive; Madison writes as a junior partner to the indispensable figure. Tone is respectful and carefully reasoned — Madison does not flatter, he argues. But the deference is real: he would not presume to tell Washington what Washington already knows. The letter should feel like a brilliant man writing to the one person he genuinely looks up to. Policy-focused framing, not emotional appeal.
response:
Mount Pleasant, Virginia
October 27th, 1788

Most esteemed General Washington,

Allow me, with the deepest respect and humility, to intrude upon your well-deserved tranquility. The ratification of our new Constitution progresses steadily – Maryland having joined the ranks of those states who have embraced this momentous change. Yet, as with any great undertaking, success is not merely *achieved* by agreement on paper, but sustained through diligent implementation and wise governance. It is regarding that latter point, sir, that my thoughts turn constantly to you, and compel me to take up my pen.

The debates which accompanied the formation of our federal compact were arduous, as befits a discussion concerning the very foundations of republican government. While reason and logic played their part in convincing many of the necessity for a more energetic Union, it is undeniable that anxieties linger. Concerns regarding the distance between government and the governed, and the potential for abuse of power, are voiced even amongst those who ultimately voted in favor of ratification. 

These concerns, I believe, stem not from flaws in the *theory* of our Constitution, but rather from a natural hesitancy to entrust so much authority to any single body of men. It is a hesitation that can only be allayed by an individual possessing a character beyond reproach – someone whose disinterestedness and unwavering commitment to the public good are demonstrably clear. 

And it is here, sir, where I confess my most profound anxiety, and my most fervent hope. The nation *needs* you, General Washington. Not merely as a symbol of our hard-won independence - though your presence will undoubtedly serve that crucial purpose – but as the guiding hand to shape this new government into the benevolent instrument envisioned by its framers. Your leadership during the Revolution was not simply military; it was moral. You commanded respect and obedience, yes, but more importantly, you commanded trust. That trust is a vital ingredient in securing the future of our republic.

I know your desire for retirement, your longing for the peaceful pursuits of Mount Vernon, are genuine and deeply felt. And believe me, sir, I would not presume to argue against such personal inclinations were it merely a question of comfort or inclination. But this is far more than that. This is about ensuring the survival of a painstakingly constructed framework, a bold experiment in self-governance.

The arguments for a strong, energetic executive are readily articulated and reasoned – but they ring hollow without a figure to *embody* those principles with the integrity and selfless devotion you alone possess.  A lesser man at the helm might confirm the anxieties of the opposition and unravel the delicate compromises achieved.  (Forgive my candor, sir - I speak only from a deep concern for our nation’s fate.)

I implore you to consider this not as a call to power, but as a solemn duty – a final service to the country that has already demanded so much of your life and sacrifice.  The weight of expectation rests heavily upon us all, but it will rest most heavily, and most justly, on your shoulders. 

With the utmost deference and respect, I remain,

Your obedient and humble servant,

James Madison.
